title: Settle the Discord library and make one documented launch path real
user_value: A user who follows the setup doc gets a bot that actually connects to Discord instead of one that dies at import with no message. Today every documented install produces a dead bot.
exists_today: Complete bot: discord_bot/bot.py (801 LOC, 20 slash commands + on_message). Working launcher discord_bot/run.py (package-style imports, proven importable). Guided token setup discord_bot/installer.py.
missing: The code is discord.py 2.x (`from discord import app_commands` bot.py:35; `bot.tree.command` at bot.py:303 and 19 more). discord_bot/requirements.txt pins py-cord; agent/docs/DISCORD_SETUP.md:12, docs/DISCORD_SETUP.md:75, discord_bot/README.md:97 all say py-cord; agent/install/setup_profiles.py:38 says discord.py. Pick discord.py (matches the code), fix requirements + 3 docs + setup_profiles, and make at least one test import the real API instead of stubbing discord.app_commands (discord_bot/tests/test_discord_bot.py:33 stubs it, which is why this was never caught).
slice_boundary: Change the dependency declaration + docs to discord.py, install it, and run `python -m discord_bot.run` against a real test guild until /ask returns an answer. Nothing else changes; discord_send keeps working throughout.

challenge.hidden_dependency: Two, both fatal to the stated slice. (1) The code is a HYBRID, not "discord.py 2.x". bot.py:595-617 uses `discord.sinks.WaveSink()`, `vc.start_recording(...)`, `vc.stop_recording()` — voice receive, which exists ONLY in py-cord; discord.py has no voice-receive API at all. So `bot.tree` + `app_commands` (20 commands) need discord.py while /listen and /stop_listen need py-cord. NO single library satisfies this file. Picking discord.py ships /listen and /stop_listen as registered slash commands that AttributeError at runtime — the exact visible-but-dead defect. The test stub hides this too (test_discord_bot.py:44 sets `_discord_stub.sinks = MagicMock()`, right next to the app_commands stub the unit did catch). (2) The in-app launch path is broken independently of the library. agent/main.py:378 does `from bot import _create_bot` after inserting discord_bot/ on sys.path, but bot.py:61-90 uses relative imports (`from .config import ...`, `from .state import ...`). Probe (app venv, resolved sys.path[0]=C:\Work\Programming\Layla\discord_bot): `FLAT IMPORT FAIL: ImportError attempted relative import with no known parent package` — and main.py:404 swallows it into `logger.warning("Discord bot failed: %s")`. discord_bot/installer.py:118 sets `discord_bot_autostart=True` and agent/install/setup_profiles.py:37-38 enables the same flag, so the guided-setup path the product itself steers users to launches nothing even with the correct library installed. Fixing requirements.txt + docs does not touch it.
challenge.reason:
EXISTS_TODAY: mostly real. bot.py (801 LOC, 22 tree commands + on_message) and run.py are genuinely complete, and run.py is a real user-reachable entry point (`python -m discord_bot.run`). But there is a SECOND user path the unit missed entirely — the `discord_bot_autostart` config flag consumed at agent/main.py:368, which is what installer.py and setup_profiles.py actually configure. That path is dead for its own reason (see hidden_dependency).

USER_VALUE: overstated. It does NOT "die at import with no message." bot.py:33-41 guards the import; run.py:75-80 prints an explicit, formatted error and exit(1). The failure is worse than silent — it is MISDIRECTING: the message says "py-cord is not installed" when py-cord being installed is the cause, and agent/docs/DISCORD_SETUP.md:228-238 has a troubleshooting entry for that exact string telling you to `pip uninstall discord.py; pip install "py-cord[voice]"`. A user who follows the doc is walked in a closed loop back into the broken state. That is the real, sharper user story.

MISSING: not the whole gap. Three things it omits — the sinks/voice-receive collision, the main.py flat-import break, and the fact that the in-product installer ALREADY chose discord.py (agent/install/feature_installer.py:40 maps `"discord.py": "discord"`; setup_profiles.py:38 lists dep `discord.py`). So this is not "docs vs code," it is two shipping install mechanisms in the same product that disagree with each other. The unit frames setup_profiles.py:38 as a thing to fix; it is the one place that is already correct.

SLICE_BOUNDARY: not shippable as written. "Change requirements + docs to discord.py, run `python -m discord_bot.run` until /ask answers" leaves (a) /listen and /stop_listen registered in Discord's command list and crashing when invoked, and (b) the autostart flag still launching nothing. Both violate TRUTH BEFORE EXPOSURE.

CORRECTED SLICE (still one shippable stage, product working throughout — discord_send is unaffected either way, it is urllib-only per agent/requirements.txt:211): commit to discord.py; remove or feature-gate the two sink-based commands so they are not registered at all when voice receive is unavailable; fix agent/main.py:378 to `from discord_bot.bot import _create_bot` with repo root on sys.path (note `transports/` is at repo ROOT, not under agent/, so bot.py:61's `from transports.base import ...` needs repo root on path too — run.py gets it from cwd, autostart may not); align requirements.txt + the 3 docs INCLUDING the self-defeating troubleshooting block at agent/docs/DISCORD_SETUP.md:228-238; de-stub the test so it imports the real `discord.app_commands` (and does not fake `discord.sinks`); verify BOTH launch paths against a live test guild.

EFFORT: "hours" is not honest. The edits are small, but the stage contains a product decision (drop voice-receive vs. keep py-cord and port 20 commands to `slash_command`/`ApplicationContext` — the latter is a rewrite, not hours), two launch paths to verify, and live-guild verification needing a Discord app, FFmpeg, and the CPU-only Layla server up for /ask. Days.

CONFIDENCE: HIGH on the sinks collision, the flat-import break (probed and reproduced), and the feature_installer/setup_profiles contradiction. MEDIUM on effort sizing, since it hinges on the voice-receive product call.

exists_today: agent/main.py:365-413 — full autostart block with a tracked daemon thread, dedicated event loop, and a clean shutdown hook at main.py:586-600. All of it correct except the import line.
missing: main.py:375-378 inserts `discord_bot/` on sys.path then does `from bot import _create_bot`; bot.py:63 is a relative import so this raises ImportError (proven by probe). title: Make the read-only boundary over Discord server-enforced, not caller-enforced
user_value: A user can trust that nobody in a Discord server can make Layla write files or run commands, even if a future Discord command is written carelessly.
exists_today: Genuinely good posture already: bot.py never passes allow_write/allow_run, so transports/base.py:268-269 defaults both to False; agent/routers/agent.py:364-371 additionally hard-forces both False for non-direct-local callers, fail-closed. Inbound is gated through a real allowlist + pairing-secret check (transports/base.py:154, called at bot.py:196 and used by every command and on_message).
missing: The bot calls localhost, so `is_direct_local` returns TRUE for it — the read-only guarantee currently rests entirely on the bot's own default arguments rather than on server enforcement. Needs an explicit transport marker (a header or a request field) that /agent treats as untrusted regardless of source IP. Latent risk only; nothing exploits it today.
depends_on: Units 1 and 2 — this is not urgent until the bot can actually run.
slice_boundary: Add the transport marker and force allow_write/allow_run False when present. Behaviour is identical today, so this ships invisibly and safely before any capability expansion.

challenge.reason: exists_today is accurate and verified on a real user path (bot.py:170 -> transports/base.py:264 with allow_write/allow_run absent; agent.py:363-371 fail-closed; allowlist gate live at bot.py:196 and :570). The unit fails on 'missing', which is not the whole gap. /agent is not the only transport-reachable endpoint: bot.py:190 _save_operator_note -> transports/base.py:304 save_learning_async -> POST /learn/, and agent/routers/learn.py:72 accepts no `request: Request` object at all, so it structurally cannot perform an origin check. is_direct_local is wired into agent.py, automation.py, autonomous.py, macros.py, openai_compat.py and settings.py but NOT learn.py. That endpoint writes to save_learning, vector_store.add_vector and memory_graph.add_node — the graph that reaches the prompt — so a Discord user can already cause a persistent write today, held back only by the allowlist, i.e. exactly the caller-enforced posture this unit exists to replace. /learn/correct (learn.py:117) is the same. Secondly, scope is understated: transports/base.py is the shared helper for slack_bot.py and telegram_bot.py as well, so the marker belongs in the shared call path and the enforcement must cover every transport-reachable router. Adding the marker to /agent alone ships a guarantee narrower than the user_value sentence claims, which is a quieter version of the same half-wiring problem. The unit does not leave anything visible-but-dead, so it is not the signature defect; effort and WIRE are both right. Corrected slice: put the marker in transports/base.py so all three transports carry it, force allow_write/allow_run False in /agent when present, and give learn.py's routes a `request: Request` plus the same gate (rejecting or downgrading transport-marked writes per operator intent). Also assert workspace_root cannot exceed the sandbox for transport callers — both call_layla_* default it to str(Path.home()); agent.py:498-501 clamps it for understand_mode and file tools are sandbox-rooted, so this is bounded, but it should be asserted in the same slice rather than split out. Still invisible and behaviour-preserving today, still shippable in hours.
challenge.hidden_dependency: POST /learn/ and /learn/correct (agent/routers/learn.py:72,117) are unguarded write endpoints reachable from every transport — learn() takes no `request: Request` so it cannot check origin, and the Discord bot already calls it via bot.py:190 -> transports/base.py:304. It writes into save_learning, vector_store and memory_graph (the prompt-reaching graph). Any transport marker must cover these, not just /agent. Secondary: transports/base.py is shared by slack_bot.py and telegram_bot.py, so this is a three-transport fix in one shared helper, not a Discord-only change.

title: Per-user rate limiting and abuse budget
user_value: One user cannot monopolize or wedge the single 3B model for everyone else in a guild. On this hardware a turn costs real seconds, so this is a practical concern the moment more than one person uses it.
exists_today: A 2-second per-channel debounce at bot.py:96-100 (`_last_call` dict, `_RATE_LIMIT_SECS = 2.0`), applied in on_message at bot.py:767.
missing: Nothing per-user, no concurrency cap, no daily budget, and slash commands (/ask, /chat_speak) bypass the debounce entirely — it is only checked in on_message. Given single-model CPU inference, two concurrent Discord users will queue behind each other with no feedback.
depends_on: Units 1 and 2. Should land before the bot is used in any guild with more than the operator in it.
slice_boundary: Extend the existing debounce to a per-user limiter and apply it to the slash commands too, with a visible 'still working on your last one' reply. Self-contained inside bot.py.

title: Fix the cluster auth handshake (keystone — unblocks the whole pillar)
user_value: Two Layla machines can actually talk. Today every cross-host request is a silent 401, so nothing downstream — sync, inference offload, drone work — can ever function regardless of config. Alone this ships no visible feature; it is the precondition for every unit below.
exists_today: Full transport is built and called: services/cluster/cluster_network.py:498 `_get_auth_headers`, routers/cluster.py:100 `_validate_cluster_auth`, heartbeat loop started at main.py:526-528. Pairing generates a real raw secret at services/cluster/cluster_pairing.py:141.
missing: Sender transmits the stored SHA256 hash; receiver hashes it again before comparing — mismatch by construction, proven by computation. Drone side (cluster_pairing.py:255-257) persists only sha256(raw) and throws the raw secret away, so it cannot produce a valid token even after the header fix. Both sides need to agree: store raw secret in the peer credential store, send raw, compare hashes. Also needs a test that does NOT monkeypatch client host to 127.0.0.1 — tests/test_cluster_e2e.py:78-87 does, which is why 100% of this passed CI while being non-functional.
slice_boundary: Change credential storage + header to raw-secret-vs-stored-hash, plus one integration test that exercises a non-localhost client IP end-to-end. Product remains exactly as working as today (cluster still off by default) but the pillar stops being structurally dead.

title: Collapse the two pairing systems into one paired-device identity
user_value: Pairing a device once, in the UI, actually makes it a peer — knowledge sync and inference offload both see it. Today a user completes the PIN flow, sees 'paired', and node_sync still has zero peers because it reads a different store.
exists_today: PIN flow: routers/pairing.py:236 initiate → :280 confirm → agent/.governance/paired_devices.json (file does not exist on this box — flow never completed). Token flow: routers/cluster.py:398/421 → services/cluster/cluster_pairing.py:110 accept_drone → cluster_config.json. Both complete and both tested.
missing: No bridge. confirm_pairing() writes paired_devices.json and never calls net.add_peer() or accept_drone(), so node_sync's net.get_online_peers() cannot return a PIN-paired device. Separately, accept_drone() OVERWRITES cluster_secret_hash on every pairing (cluster_pairing.py:144-146), so pairing a second device silently invalidates the first — only one peer can ever authenticate. Needs a per-peer credential rather than one global cluster secret.
slice_boundary: Make the PIN flow the single front door: on confirm, mint a per-peer secret, store it in ONE store, and register the peer with cluster_network. Retire /cluster/pair's token path to an internal detail. Ships as: pair two machines in the UI and see each other in /cluster/peers — a real, demonstrable capability, with sync still off.

challenge.reason:
Core claims verified: confirm_pairing (routers/pairing.py:316-332) writes only paired_devices.json with no add_peer/accept_drone call, and accept_drone (cluster_pairing.py:144-146) unconditionally overwrites cluster_secret_hash so peer #2 invalidates peer #1. Both flows have real UI callers (ui/components/pairing.js:113/177/203 and ui/components/cluster.js:180/301). node_sync has a real caller (agent/main.py:574-576).

But the unit is wrong on one point and incomplete on two. (1) INVERTED: user_value says inference offload "reads a different store" from the PIN flow. services/llm/inference_router.py:600-601 imports _load_paired_devices from routers.pairing and filters on permissions.inference_offload — offload ALREADY reads the PIN store and is its only working consumer. Only knowledge sync reads the cluster store. A collapse that makes cluster_network the registry must explicitly migrate _get_cluster_peers() or it relocates the store out from under the one thing that works — the exact signature defect. inference_router is not mentioned anywhere in the unit. (2) zeroconf is absent from the app venv (pyproject.toml:163-165 puts it in the optional `network` extra; import fails in .venv). mDNS is the front door for BOTH paths: pairing.js renders Pair buttons from /pairing/peers, and _get_cluster_peers() calls get_discovered_peers() first and returns [] on ImportError. A perfect bridge still yields zero peers on a default install, so slice_boundary as written is not demonstrable. (3) Live agent/cluster_config.json (path printed by probe) holds ~700 peers named lifecycle-drone-* generated by tests/test_cluster_e2e.py:490 — the test suite writes the production config, each accept_drone rotating the global secret; cluster_enabled is False. The slice's acceptance demo ("see each other in /cluster/peers") would render hundreds of ghost drones.

Effort: per-peer credentials change _require_auth across /heartbeat, /task/submit, /sync/push, /sync/pull, /peers plus drone_worker, on top of offload-consumer migration, packaging zeroconf into the default install, cluster_config test isolation, ghost purge, and a two-machine verification loop. Weeks, not days.

Direction (WIRE, PIN flow as single front door) is correct and should be kept; the scope is what fails.
challenge.hidden_dependency: zeroconf is only an optional `network` extra and is not installed — mDNS discovery gates BOTH the PIN pairing UI and _get_cluster_peers(), so no peer is pairable or offloadable on a default install regardless of the identity bridge. Compounded by: services/llm/inference_router.py:600 is an unscoped third consumer already reading paired_devices.json, and the live agent/cluster_config.json is polluted with ~700 test-planted peers (tests/test_cluster_e2e.py:490) because the suite writes the production config.

title: Remote access to the one brain (Tailscale + hardened API + phone-usable UI)
user_value: THE thing the operator actually asked for: open Layla from a laptop or phone anywhere and continue the same conversation, with the same memory, mid-thread. One brain, one writer — no replication, no conflicts, no divergence. This is the genuinely-useful minimal version of 'multi-device' and it sidesteps the hard distributed-systems problem entirely.
exists_today: Most of it. services/infrastructure/tailscale_manager.py with start/stop/status/funnel endpoints at routers/system.py:743-790; remote auth middleware (main.py:1217), endpoint allowlist derived from remote_mode (main.py:1150), per-IP rate limiting (main.py:1324), audit logging (main.py:1247); config keys remote_enabled/remote_mode/remote_api_key/remote_rate_limit_per_minute all present in live config; a service worker at ui/sw.js.
missing: Unverified end-to-end and never framed as a product. remote_enabled=False and tailscale_enabled=False live; no probe I ran proves a phone can reach a chat turn. Unknown whether the default remote_mode='observe' allowlist even permits the chat endpoint — if it does not, the feature is on-paper only. Needs: one honest end-to-end verification, a setup path that is not hand-editing JSON, and a check that the UI is usable at phone width.
slice_boundary: First slice is investigation, not code: turn remote_enabled on behind a token, reach /chat from a second device, and record what actually breaks. Only then scope the build. Do not surface a 'Remote access' UI until a real device has held a conversation through it — TRUTH BEFORE EXPOSURE. Likely the highest value-per-hour unit in this whole area.

challenge.reason: "exists_today" overstates and "missing" understates. Verified against the code, not the description: (1) GET / is NOT in the interactive allowlist (main.py:1150-1203) though the UI is served there (main.py:1407) — a phone hitting the bare host 403s; (2) a probe of the UI's real call graph (68 JS files, 109 distinct endpoints, evaluated with main.py's own matcher) shows 63 of 109 endpoints denied even in the most permissive built-in mode, including /history, /memory/*, /operator/profile, /onboarding/*, /platform/*, /search, /skills — chat works, the app around it does not; (3) default remote_mode="observe" allows only /wakeup, /project_discovery, /health, so /agent is denied — the unit's open question resolves to "on-paper only at defaults"; (4) no credential can exist and no UI creates one: live agent/runtime_config.json has tunnel_token_hash=None and remote_api_key=None, so check_auth returns "no auth configured" (403), and zero UI files reference /remote/token/rotate or /remote/tailscale/*; (5) DECISIVE — the codebase's signature defect is ALREADY SHIPPED here: agent/ui/index.html:24 displays a remote-key banner instructing the user to enter remote_api_key from runtime_config.json, but services/safety/auth.py:check_auth only honors that key when allow_legacy_remote_api_key is true, which is absent from live config (default False). Following the on-screen instruction yields 401 forever. A remote-access UI element is visible-but-dead today, which is precisely the condition that forces holds=false. What IS genuinely wired: POST /agent streaming (app.js:391) with Bearer injection reaching it via both the global fetch patch (bootstrap.js:36, invoked at :250) and services/api.js:83 through core/compat.js:170.
challenge.hidden_dependency: EventSource cannot carry an Authorization header, and ui/components/models.js and ui/components/setup.js use it for model-download and setup progress — those features are structurally unreachable remotely no matter what the allowlist or token does, and need a query-token or WebSocket path first. Secondly, the real work item is not Tailscale (whose endpoints at routers/system.py:743-790 are real) but curating the remote allowlist against the UI's actual call graph, or inverting it to a deny-list — a security/behaviour decision, not a config toggle.

title: Sync what a user would actually notice: conversations, not just learnings
user_value: A thread started on the desktop is readable — and continuable — on the laptop. This is what 'multi-device' means to a user. No mechanism in the repo does this today.
exists_today: Only the plumbing shape: node_sync.py's timestamp-since + content_hash dedup + pending_sync buffer + dead-letter handling is genuinely solid engineering and the right template. The tables exist (`conversations`, `conversation_messages`, `conversation_messages_fts`).
missing: Everything conversation-specific. node_sync touches ONLY the `learnings` table (get_learnings_since / import_learnings / the /cluster/sync push+pull endpoints all hardcode it). No change log, no per-row versioning, no conflict policy, no FTS reindex on import, no handling of `parent_id`/`forked_at_message_id` referential integrity across nodes. Two writable nodes editing one conversation is a real distributed-systems problem this codebase has not attempted.
depends_on: Fix the cluster auth handshake; Collapse the two pairing systems; and the remote-access investigation, which may make this unnecessary
slice_boundary: Do not start until the remote-access unit reports back. If reaching one brain remotely works, this is largely redundant for a single-user product and should stay deferred. If it is still wanted, the honest first slice is one-way replication to a read-only mirror (desktop is authoritative, laptop can read history but not write) — that ships something working and needs no conflict resolution.

challenge.reason: Ordering/DEFER is right, but the facts are wrong in decision-changing ways. (1) "No FTS reindex on import" is FALSE — conversation_messages_fts is external-content FTS5 maintained by three triggers (agent/layla/memory/migrations.py:525-548); a plain INSERT reindexes for free. (2) "parent_id/forked_at_message_id referential integrity across nodes" is near-free — both conversations.id and conversation_messages.id are TEXT PRIMARY KEY holding str(uuid.uuid4()) (agent/layla/memory/conversations.py:92,173,373,391), so IDs are already globally unique; integrity reduces to importing parents before children. (3) "Two writable nodes editing one conversation is a real distributed-systems problem" is irrelevant to the proposed slice: messages are immutable (repo-wide zero `UPDATE conversation_messages`; only INSERT/DELETE), and only conversations metadata mutates, which already carries updated_at (last-writer-wins suffices for a mirror). Because ID remapping, search reindex, and edit conflicts are all pre-solved by existing schema, the first slice is days, not weeks. Correctly verified: node_sync does hardcode `learnings` (node_sync.py:65-171) and /cluster/sync/{push,pull} (routers/cluster.py:263,315) hardcode it in raw SQL — no conversation replication exists. Better than claimed: the plumbing HAS a live caller (main.py:574-576 starts NodeSync; layla/scheduler/registry.py:191 registers sync_now), so it is not merely a shape.
challenge.hidden_dependency: The "read-only mirror" slice has no mechanism to BE read-only. There is no provenance/origin_node column on `conversations` (verified absent) and no read-only or disabled-composer affordance anywhere in agent/ui/components/conversations.js or input.js. A replicated thread would render as an ordinary writable conversation: the user types into it on the laptop, a reply is generated and stored locally, and the two nodes diverge silently with zero conflict detection — the exact visible-but-dead defect this audit hunts. The slice MUST include (a) an origin/mirror column on conversations, (b) a composer lock plus a visible "mirrored from <node>, read-only" banner in the UI, or it ships half-wired. Secondary: import must be ordered parents-before-children (or tolerate dangling parent_id) since fork rows reference conversations/messages that may arrive later.

title: Reframe Syncthing: drop it as a DB-sync mechanism, keep it for files
user_value: Removes a documented footgun. The current setup guide instructs the user to file-sync a live SQLite database and then hand-delete .sync-conflict files — that is a path to silently losing memory. Repointed at the knowledge/workspace folders it becomes safe and genuinely useful.
exists_today: services/infrastructure/syncthing_sync.py (259 lines, complete REST client, never logs the API key), routers/sync.py mounted at main.py:1092, ui/components/sync.js with a correct gate on syncthing_api_key, palette entry at ui/main.js:632. Zero production importers — nothing in the tree calls it except the router.
missing: Nothing technical is broken; the TARGET is wrong. The setup guide (routers/sync.py setup_guide, step 5) points the synced folder at the directory containing layla.db. Needs repointing at the knowledge-watcher folder and sandbox workspace, guide rewritten, and an explicit warning against syncing the DB. Also: the sandbox_root is empty per prior findings, so the file-sync value depends on that being fixed first.
depends_on: NONE (but its value depends on sandbox_root pointing at real files)
slice_boundary: Rewrite the setup guide to target document/workspace folders and add an explicit 'never sync layla.db' warning. Guide-and-copy only, no behaviour change — the product gets strictly safer the moment it ships. Do NOT cut the module; the operator wants this pillar and the code is sound.

title: Stop advertising over mDNS when every consumer is switched off
user_value: A privacy and honesty fix: the machine currently broadcasts its identity, hostname and hardware tier on the LAN by default while clustering, sync, offload and pairing are all disabled, so nothing can act on a discovered peer.
exists_today: main.py:420 starts mDNS whenever `mdns_enabled` is true — and it defaults to True, unlike cluster_enabled (False), which gates every downstream consumer. services/cluster/mdns_discovery.py is 470 lines of working discovery.
missing: A coherent gate. Discovery should run when the user is trying to pair or when clustering is on — not unconditionally on every standalone install. Note this is the same class of defect main.py:537-552 already fixed for the drone/queen worker threads; the reasoning in that comment applies verbatim here and was not carried across.
slice_boundary: Gate mDNS start on (cluster_enabled OR an active pairing session), keeping /pairing/start able to bring it up on demand so the pairing flow still works. Strictly reduces default network exposure; no working feature is lost.

title: Make one pack actually execute (close the venv/flag contradiction)
user_value: With the execute flag on, the user can run an installed pack and get its stdout back in chat. Today this is impossible under every default configuration — the tool always refuses or errors.
exists_today: Full runner with path confinement, env allowlist, timeout, stdin forwarding: agent/services/skills/skill_sandbox.py run_entry_point. Venv provisioning: create_venv/install_dependencies, same file. Executor + registry bookkeeping: agent/layla/tools/impl/general.py:196-292. Install-side provisioning call: agent/services/skills/skill_packs.py:~245.
missing: install only calls create_venv when skill_venv_enabled=true (default False, config_schema.py:203) but run_entry_point unconditionally requires the venv interpreter. Either provision the venv whenever skill_packs_execute_enabled is on, or let run_entry_point fall back to the host interpreter for a pack declaring zero dependencies. Also needs one real reference pack (valid manifest + entry_point) to prove the path, and a self-healing create_venv-on-first-run so packs installed before the fix are not permanently dead.
slice_boundary: Fix the venv precondition + ship one runnable reference pack + a test that installs it and asserts a non-empty stdout with exit_code 0. No UI, no catalog. Product is strictly better: the tool goes from always-fails to works-when-enabled.
area: Skill packs / plugin SDK — discover → install → execute → tool routing

title: Rebuild the bundled skill packs — the current five are a false start
user_value: Layla ships with 2-3 packs the user can actually list and run out of the box, instead of five names that cannot be installed, validated, or executed.
exists_today: skill_packs/{cad_cam,engineering,research,translation,writing}/manifest.json — one file each, no code, no knowledge files. Schema + validator that rejects them: agent/services/skills/skill_manifest.py.
missing: All five fail validate_manifest on two counts (probed): no entry_point, and a `name` with spaces/slashes that violates the ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$ rule. They also live in skill_packs/ which nothing reads — list_installed only scans .layla/skill_packs_installed/. Needs: schema-valid manifests, a real entry_point per pack, and a first-run seeding step that copies bundled packs into INSTALLED_DIR and registers them. Recommend replacing all five with two genuinely useful packs rather than patching stubs.
slice_boundary: Delete the five stubs, add two valid packs with working entry points, add the seed-on-first-run step, assert via list_skill_packs that runnable=true for both. Ships a working product: discovery goes from zero to two real entries.
depends_on: Unit 1 (a pack that cannot run is not worth seeding)

challenge.hidden_dependency: run_skill_pack cannot execute ANY seeded pack, because execution hard-requires a per-pack venv that only the git-install path creates — and that path is off by default. Chain, all verified in source: (1) agent/services/skills/skill_sandbox.py:169-171 `run_entry_point` starts with `python = _venv_python(pack_name)` and returns `{"ok": False, "stderr": "venv Python not found: ..."}` if it does not exist. There is no sys.executable fallback. (2) `create_venv` has exactly ONE production caller — agent/services/skills/skill_packs.py:252, inside `install_from_git`, guarded by `if _cfg.get("skill_venv_enabled", False)`. Default is False (agent/config_schema.py:203). A seeding step that COPIES bundled packs into INSTALLED_DIR (what the slice proposes) never touches install_from_git, so no venv is ever provisioned and every run fails at line 171. (3) Second independent gate: `skill_packs_execute_enabled` default False (config_schema.py:205) makes run_skill_pack refuse before dispatch (impl/general.py:215), AND makes `installed_summary_for_prompt` return "" (skill_packs.py:327) so the model is never told the packs exist and will never choose the tool. (4) The slice's own acceptance test is a broken probe: `runnable` in list_installed_readonly (skill_packs.py:~305) is literally `bool(isinstance(entry_point, str) and entry_point.strip())` — it echoes a manifest string and never checks the file exists, the venv exists, or the flag is on. Asserting runnable=true would pass on two packs that cannot run.
challenge.reason:
exists_today and missing are factually confirmed, but the gap is under-scoped and the slice_boundary is not shippable as written.

CONFIRMED by probe (ran validate_manifest against the real files, resolved path C:\Work\Programming\Layla\skill_packs): all five manifests fail on exactly the two counts claimed — name violates ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$ ('CAD / CAM', 'Engineering workflows', ...) and entry_point is absent. Each pack dir contains one manifest.json and nothing else (find returned 5 files total). CONFIRMED that nothing reads skill_packs/: the only production consumers are INSTALLED_DIR = REPO_ROOT/.layla/skill_packs_installed (skill_packs.py:15) via list_installed / list_installed_readonly / run_skill_pack / routers/system.py:811. No source path reference to the repo-root skill_packs/ directory exists outside docs. The live INSTALLED_DIR on disk is EMPTY — zero packs installed, so discovery today genuinely returns 0.

Why holds=false: "missing" names schema-valid manifests, an entry_point, and a seed step. That is necessary and insufficient. It omits the venv provisioning requirement entirely (see hidden_dependency), and it omits both consent flags. Execute the slice exactly as written and you get: list_skill_packs reports two packs with runnable=true, the model is never told they exist, and if the operator flips skill_packs_execute_enabled the tool fails with "venv Python not found". That is a feature visible-but-dead — the precise failure mode this audit exists to prevent.

Corrected slice that actually ships working: seeding must go through a provisioning path, not a copy. Either (a) extend/refactor install_from_git into an install_from_dir that runs the same manifest-validate → register → create_venv sequence for bundled packs, or (b) make run_entry_point fall back to the host interpreter when a pack declares zero dependencies (defensible: a dependency-free pack needs no dependency isolation, and the module docstring is explicit that the venv is NOT a security boundary — the consent flags are). Option (b) is the smaller change and lets the two bundled packs be dependency-free by design, which they should be on a CPU-only local box. Then: make `runnable` mean runnable — have list_installed_readonly verify the entry_point file exists on disk and (venv path or fallback) is available, rather than echoing a manifest string. Then decide the default of skill_packs_execute_enabled for FIRST-PARTY bundled packs; leaving it False is a legitimate security choice, but then the honest slice ships "discovery works, execution requires one documented opt-in", and the acceptance criterion must be an end-to-end run_skill_pack returning exit_code 0 with the flag on — not a runnable=true field read.

depends_on is right (Unit 1 first). effort "days" stands, but the days are now mostly in the provisioning/fallback decision and the runnable-means-runnable fix, not in authoring two packs. confidence HIGH on the defect diagnosis is warranted; confidence HIGH on the slice as written was not.

title: Give the model something to discover — pack summary reaches the prompt
user_value: The user asks "what can you do with X" and Layla names an installed pack and offers to run it, instead of never mentioning packs at all.
exists_today: installed_summary_for_prompt() in skill_packs.py (correct, complete) and its injection site at agent/services/agent/llm_decision.py:408-419. list_skill_packs tool fully registered.
missing: Nothing structural — it is gated on skill_packs_execute_enabled AND on at least one runnable pack existing, and both are false today. This unit is the verification that after Units 1-2 the hint actually lands: probe the assembled prompt and assert the pack id appears, then confirm list_skill_packs starts appearing in tool_outcomes (0 rows today).
slice_boundary: One assertion-bearing probe on the composed decision prompt plus a golden-eval case that a pack-shaped question selects list_skill_packs. No code change expected — if the probe fails, the fix belongs here.

challenge.reason: "missing: nothing structural" is falsified. The prompt hint is appended to prompt_context at llm_decision.py:408-419 BEFORE get_tools_for_goal runs at line 421, and is never intersected with the resulting valid_tools set. A live probe against the real config (tool_routing_enabled=True, tool_visibility_cap=15) shows the intent router drops both skill-pack tools for exactly the phrasing in this unit's user_value: 'what can you do with pdfs' -> n=12, run_skill_pack False, list_skill_packs False; 'can you summarize a csv for me' -> n=9, both False. They only survive when the user literally says "skill pack" ('list my skill packs' -> both True). rg for "skill" across services/tools/intent_router.py, tool_policy.py and toolchain_graph.py returns zero hits: there is no skill-pack intent rule anywhere. So after Units 1-2 the assembled prompt would tell the model "run one with the run_skill_pack tool, pack=<id>" while run_skill_pack is not in valid_tools, and llm_decision.py:624 (`if action == "tool" and tool and tool not in valid_tools: tool = None`) silently discards the model's correct choice. Advertised, obeyed, dropped — the codebase's signature defect. Two further errors: (1) the hint names run_skill_pack, not list_skill_packs, so the proposed acceptance signal measures a tool the hint never mentions; (2) list_skill_packs is NOT gated on skill_packs_execute_enabled (impl/general.py:181-193 reports the flag rather than refusing), so it does not depend on Units 1-2 and a probe built on it could pass green while the advertised path stays dead. The verification-only slice_boundary is therefore not shippable as written. What is genuinely real: installed_summary_for_prompt (skill_packs.py:318) is correct and complete, its injection site exists, both tools are in VALID_TOOLS, and the fast path at routers/agent.py:658 only catches trivial greetings so a real question does reach llm_decision.
challenge.hidden_dependency: The intent router / tool visibility cap (services/tools/intent_router.py, tool_policy.py, toolchain_graph.py, tool_visibility_cap default 15) has no skill-pack rule, so run_skill_pack and list_skill_packs are filtered out of valid_tools for any capability-shaped question that does not contain the literal words "skill pack". Two code changes are required before the verification probe can be meaningful: (a) a routing rule that admits the skill-pack tools when a runnable pack is installed and execution is enabled, and (b) coupling the hint to valid_tools — compute valid_tools first and suppress or reword the hint when run_skill_pack was filtered out, so the prompt can never advertise a tool the decision loop will silently drop at llm_decision.py:624.

title: Surface packs in the existing kit marketplace (cheapest exposure win)
user_value: The user opens the kit marketplace they already have and installs a skill pack in one click, instead of the current curl-only POST /skill_packs/install.
exists_today: Working marketplace UI: agent/ui/components/marketplace.js (⌘K → "Kit marketplace"), backed by GET /kits/catalog and POST /kits/install (agent/routers/kits.py). install_kit ALREADY has a git_url branch that calls install_from_git (kit_catalog.py:~88).
missing: Zero KIT_CATALOG entries carry a git_url — all 7 are feature kits, so the pack branch has never executed. Needs pack entries in the catalog, and installed_status() extended to consult the skill registry for pack kits (it currently only checks feature flags + packages, so a pack kit would always badge as not-installed).

title: Make the registry readable — health, last run, and provenance stop being write-only
user_value: The user can see which packs are installed, where each came from, when it last ran, and whether it errored — instead of a bare directory listing.
exists_today: Complete SQLite CRUD with health/last_run/permissions/git_url columns: agent/services/skills/skill_registry.py. Write side is genuinely wired: register() on install, update_last_run + update_health on run (impl/general.py:284-290).

Needs the endpoint to merge registry rows with list_installed_readonly() and expose health/last_run/git_url.

depends_on: NONE (independently shippable, but only interesting once packs exist)

title: Dedicated skill-pack UI: install by URL, list, run, remove
user_value: The user manages packs from the app — paste a git URL to install, see health, run one, remove it — without touching curl or a config file.
exists_today: Every backend call it needs: POST /skill_packs/install, POST /skill_packs/remove, GET /skill_packs, and the run_skill_pack tool. Overlay-shell pattern to copy from: marketplace.js.
missing: The entire frontend. Confirmed zero matches for "skill_pack" anywhere under agent/ui. Also needs an in-UI toggle for skill_packs_execute_enabled with an honest warning (the runner is dependency isolation, not a security jail — the module docstring is explicit about this and the UI must repeat it).
slice_boundary: One overlay component reusing the marketplace shell, wired to the four existing endpoints. Do NOT build this before Units 1 and 5 land — a UI over a runner that always errors is exactly the pattern the operator is trying to stop.

challenge.reason: "exists_today" is wrong on two of the four backend calls it claims. (1) There is NO HTTP endpoint to run a pack: run_skill_pack is a model-facing tool only (agent/layla/tools/impl/general.py:196), registered in the tool domain/args/permission tables; `rg dispatch_tool|execute_tool|run_tool` across agent/routers/*.py and agent/main.py returns zero hits, so no router can dispatch any tool. A UI Run button would have to route through the 3B chat model picking the tool — unreliable on this box. A new POST /skill_packs/run is required. (2) "See health" has no data source: GET /skill_packs returns list_installed() (skill_packs.py:125), which is the raw manifest JSON read off disk — no health_status, no last_run, no venv state. skill_registry holds those columns (run_skill_pack is their only writer, per its own inline comment) but `rg skill_registry agent/routers/ agent/main.py` returns zero — the registry is not exposed over HTTP at all. Also a third gap: the unit names one toggle, but the run path needs two. skill_venv_enabled defaults False (config_schema.py:203); install provisions a venv only when it is True, and run_entry_point returns "venv Python not found" at skill_sandbox.py:170 when it is missing. A user flipping only skill_packs_execute_enabled installs a pack and every run hard-fails. skill_deps_require_pinned also defaults True, so a typical community git URL fails install with "unpinned dependencies" unless surfaced. Verified as real: GET /skill_packs (system.py:811), POST /skill_packs/install (:818), POST /skill_packs/remove (:833), all reachable — /skill_packs is in the main.py:1193 localhost route allowlist. agent/ui has zero skill_pack matches (frontend gap confirmed). Live .layla/skill_packs_installed/ is empty: zero packs ever installed. Recommendation stays BUILD and effort stays days — the extra backend is a thin run endpoint plus list-enrichment, not a new subsystem — but the scope must be corrected first or the stage ships a Run button wired to nothing and an empty health column, the exact defect this scoping exists to stop.

title: Enforce declared pack permissions at execution
user_value: A pack that declared only read_memory cannot quietly write files or open sockets — the permission list in its manifest becomes a real constraint the user can trust.
exists_today: VALID_PERMISSIONS vocabulary + validation in skill_manifest.py:32-126; permissions stored in the registry (skill_packs.py:225) and surfaced by list_installed_readonly.
missing: No enforcement whatsoever — run_entry_point never reads the permission list. Grep across agent/services/skills and impl/general.py shows permissions touched only in validation, storage, and display paths. On a CPU-only Windows box real enforcement is hard (no namespaces), so the honest options are: (a) present permissions as a consent prompt at install time, or (b) drop the vocabulary rather than imply a guarantee that does not exist. Investigate before building.
slice_boundary: Start with the install-time consent prompt — it is truthful and cheap. Only pursue actual runtime enforcement after establishing what is achievable on Windows without a container.

challenge.reason: The unit treats permissions as an unenforced gate awaiting investigation. Re-reading the code shows it is not a gate at all, and the investigation has already concluded. Three things break it: (1) exists_today is real but the decision is already made and documented — skill_sandbox.py's module docstring and agent/docs/SKILL_PACKS.md:344-352 explicitly state packs run at full operator privilege with no filesystem jail and no network namespace, and instruct the user to treat install as running a downloaded script. (2) The hidden dependency: a pack subprocess has NO channel back into Layla. run_entry_point passes only _filtered_env() plus LAYLA_SKILL_PACK and LAYLA_PACK_DIR; env_extra has zero production callers (repo-wide rg finds only skill_sandbox.py:157/202 and unrelated local names in test_operator_state_isolation.py). So read_memory, write_memory, voice and browser name capabilities Layla never grants and cannot revoke, while read_file/write_file/network/shell are ambient OS privileges Layla cannot withdraw without a container. The permission list is not an unenforced constraint — it is a descriptive label, and there is nothing on either side to enforce. (3) The slice_boundary fails the operator's own rule. Install-time consent already exists and is stronger than what is proposed: skill_venv_enabled and skill_packs_execute_enabled both default off and are absent from the live agent/runtime_config.json, both are blocked from remote /settings writes, run_skill_pack is registered dangerous/approval-required, and marketplace.js:121-141 already runs a plan-then-confirm dialog before install_from_git. The only delta the slice would add is rendering the permission strings in that dialog — surfacing a list that constrains nothing, which is precisely the visible-but-dead defect this audit exists to prevent, and it would make the product LESS truthful than today's blunt 'this is a downloaded script' copy. Live-data check: layla.db has zero tables matching '%skill%' — the installed_skills registry has never been created and no pack has ever been installed, so this unit also has no current users. Recommend CUT of the enforcement and permission-consent work. The honest residue is a labeling change, not a roadmap unit: rename the manifest field's presentation to advisory metadata (declared capabilities, informational) in SKILL_PACKS.md, the registry display, and list_installed_readonly output, and drop the 'Permission allow-list' bullet from the Security Model section at SKILL_PACKS.md:341 where it reads as a runtime control rather than the vocabulary check it actually is. That is hours, ships working, and improves truthfulness with no new surface.
challenge.hidden_dependency: Skill packs have no IPC channel back into Layla — run_entry_point gives the subprocess only a filtered env plus LAYLA_SKILL_PACK and LAYLA_PACK_DIR, and env_extra has zero production callers. Half the permission vocabulary (read_memory, write_memory, voice, browser) therefore names capabilities Layla never grants, and the other half are ambient OS privileges it cannot revoke without a container. Enforcement is not hard on Windows — it is undefined, because there is no mediated capability to gate.

title: Fix the two off-by-one directory roots (markdown skills + plugin loader)
user_value: Dropping a SKILL.md into skills/ or a plugin into plugins/ actually takes effect. Today both are silently ignored — the documented workflow does nothing.
exists_today: markdown_skills.load_markdown_skills_prompt, correctly called from agent/layla/skills/registry.py:601 with the skills_enabled gate. plugin_loader.load_plugins, correctly called from agent/main.py:236 at startup, plus route_helpers.py:23 and system_doctor.py:315.
missing: Both compute REPO_ROOT one directory short of the repo. markdown_skills.py:16-17 names it AGENT_DIR but resolves to agent/services, making REPO_ROOT=agent and the scan base agent/skills (empty) instead of repo-root skills/ (which has the README telling users to put files there). Probed: prompt length 0. plugin_loader.py:11 has the same shape, scanning agent/plugins (a cookiecutter _template only) instead of repo-root plugins/ where plugins/example/plugin.yaml lives. Probed: load_plugins({}) returns 0 skills / 0 tools / 0 capabilities and example_skill is absent from SKILLS. Two one-line fixes plus a regression test asserting the resolved path equals the repo root.
slice_boundary: Fix both constants, add a test that asserts the resolved scan directory is the repo root (not merely that loading succeeds — that is what let this survive), and confirm plugins/example loads. Independently shippable, no dependencies, immediate user-visible effect.

title: Offload must fail silently to local, never speak its error to the user
user_value: With offload on and the gaming PC asleep, unplugged, or rejecting auth, the user gets Layla's normal (local) answer instead of the raw string "Cluster offload to Beefy failed: [WinError 10061]..." presented as her reply. This is the difference between a degrading feature and a broken product.
exists_today: inference_router.py:828-844 already validates the peer reply and returns None on empty text; llm_gateway.py:1060-1062 already proceeds locally when out is None. The fallback contract is designed and half-honored.
missing: run_completion_cluster (inference_router.py:682-684) manufactures {"choices":[{"message":{"content": "Cluster offload to X failed: ..."}}]} on any exception, and the caller's only acceptance test is 'text is non-empty' (line 840). Needs run_completion_cluster to return None (or raise) on transport failure, plus a caller-side check that the reply is a genuine completion, not a synthesized error envelope. Same defect in the streaming generator at line 672, which yields "" and calls it a stream.
slice_boundary: Fix the error envelope and the acceptance check only. Offload stays default-off and stays non-streaming; nothing new is surfaced. The product is strictly better: the one config flag that could poison an answer no longer can.
area: CLUSTERING — potato→gaming-PC inference offload, drone side, pairing UX, discovery

title: Tell the truth about hardware tier, or the gaming PC is never chosen
user_value: A user who anchors a real GPU box gets it actually selected. Today the beefy machine advertises itself as 'cpu' and the potato correctly refuses to offload to an apparent equal — the feature declines to work while behaving exactly as designed.
exists_today: detect_hardware_tier (mdns_discovery.py:84-98), the rank table and outrank guard (inference_router.py:815-822), advertisement of hardware_tier in the mDNS TXT record (mdns_discovery.py:283), and a separate correct VRAM detector in install/setup_wizard.py:181-199.
missing: Two things. mdns_discovery.py:89 uses `.total_mem`; torch's attribute is `.total_memory`, so the probe always raises and is swallowed at line 96. And line 270 prefers cfg["hardware_tier"], which defaults to "cpu" in the schema, so detection never runs unless the setup wizard wrote a value. Needs the attribute fixed and the config value treated as an override of a detected default, not a silent winner. Reconcile with setup_wizard's detector rather than keeping two.
slice_boundary: Fix detection and precedence; expose the resolved tier in the existing /cluster/status and /pairing/status payloads so the user can SEE what each box thinks it is. No behavior change until offload is enabled, so this ships safely on its own.
area: CLUSTERING — potato→gaming-PC inference offload, drone side, pairing UX, discovery

title: Only ever offload to a peer the user actually paired with
user_value: The user's prompts — the most private data in the product — go only to a machine they explicitly approved. Today, enabling one config flag sends full prompts to any device on the LAN that advertises a GPU tier over mDNS.
exists_today: _get_cluster_peers (inference_router.py:591-611) already does exactly the right thing: intersects discovered peers with routers.pairing._load_paired_devices and requires permissions.inference_offload. The permission is granted at pairing time (pairing.py:326) and editable via PATCH /pairing/{id}/permissions.
missing: The live path doesn't call it. try_cluster_offload_first (inference_router.py:817) calls get_best_peer_for_inference(), which filters by tier alone (mdns_discovery.py:392-405). _get_cluster_peers is reached only from the zero-caller run_completion_with_fallback and from get_cluster_status. Pure caller-swap: select from _get_cluster_peers, then apply the outrank test.
depends_on: NONE (independent of the tier fix, but pointless to demo before it)
slice_boundary: Swap the selector in try_cluster_offload_first and delete/retire run_completion_with_fallback, whose failure-fallback role is now subsumed. One function's peer source changes; the feature is still default-off. TRUTH BEFORE EXPOSURE: this must land before any UI toggle exists.

title: One real pairing handshake, and a drone-join button that works
user_value: The user opens Layla on the gaming PC, clicks 'Generate pairing token', types that token plus the queen's address on the laptop, and the two machines are genuinely paired — each knowing about the other. Today that button produces a 422 and the alternative flow records a trust relationship the other machine never hears about.
exists_today: The real handshake is complete on the queen side: localhost-only token mint (cluster.py:397-419), token validation and accept_drone (cluster.py:421-452, cluster_pairing.py:55-205), Bearer auth with hmac.compare_digest (cluster.py:112-127). The drone-side client ClusterPairing.request_pairing (cluster_pairing.py:206-278) is written and correct-looking. The UI panel, token field, address field and result element all exist (ui/components/cluster.js:290-322).
missing: No endpoint calls request_pairing, and the UI posts the wrong shape: cluster.js:301 sends {queen_address, token} to its own POST /cluster/pair, which expects {pairing_token, instance_id, name, address, hardware_tier} (cluster.py:91-96). Needs a drone-side POST /cluster/join that takes {queen_address, token}, calls request_pairing, and persists the result — plus the UI pointed at it. Separately: the /pairing/* PIN flow is a false start as an AUTH mechanism (pairing.py:236-334 mints and consumes the PIN on one machine, and the shared_secret it stores is never transmitted or checked); keep its device-list and permissions UI, but rebuild its trust step on this token handshake rather than maintaining two pairing systems.
slice_boundary: Ship the drone-join endpoint + UI fix first, so pairing genuinely works end-to-end on the /cluster/ system. Converging /pairing/* onto it is a separate follow-on slice — do not start it until the offload path reads its device list, or you will move the goalposts mid-flight.

challenge.hidden_dependency:
The credential the handshake returns can never be used, and the addresses it stores point nowhere. Three defects, all inside this slice:

(1) BEARER DOUBLE-HASH — cluster auth is dead cross-machine. cluster_network.py:498-507 `_get_auth_headers` sends `cluster_secret_hash` (a SHA-256 hex digest) as the Bearer token; the queen's `_validate_cluster_auth` (cluster.py:118-124) computes `sha256(token)` and compares it to that same stored hash. sha256(H) != H, so every authenticated call (/heartbeat, /task/submit, /sync/*, /peers) 401s from any non-localhost caller. The tunnel fallback has the identical bug: it sends `tunnel_token_hash` and `validate_token` (tunnel_auth.py:71-74) hashes it again. Worse, this is not a one-line fix: `request_pairing` (cluster_pairing.py:255-257) discards the raw secret and persists only `sha256(raw)`, so the drone has no raw credential to send even after fixing the header. Fixing it requires a storage-format change on the drone side (persist raw secret, keep hash on the queen) plus a config-schema/migration touch. Every existing test passes because they all run against one localhost TestClient, which takes the localhost bypass at cluster.py:104-106 and never exercises the token path.

(2) ADDRESSES RESOLVE TO NOWHERE without Tailscale. `request_pairing` (cluster_pairing.py:221-227) derives `drone_address` solely from `tailscale_manager.get_connection_url`, which returns None when tailscale isn't on PATH — so the queen records the drone with `address: ""`, and cluster_network's `_sync_pull`/`_post` early-return on `if not peer.address`. Symmetrically, `accept_drone` (cluster_pairing.py:179-186) falls back to `"http://127.0.0.1:8000"` and the drone stores it via `result.get("queen_address", queen_address)` (line 265) — `.get` with a default only fires when the key is ABSENT, and the key is always present, so the address the USER TYPED is discarded in favour of the queen's self-reported localhost. The drone ends up pointed at itself. No LAN-IP fallback exists anywhere in the path.

(3) SECRET ROTATION ON EVERY PAIR — `accept_drone` (cluster_pairing.py:143-146) regenerates `cluster_secret_hash` even when one already exists, silently invalidating every previously paired drone. Harmless at N=1, a latent data-loss-class bug the moment a second machine or a re-pair happens.

Consequence for the slice as scoped: shipping only the /cluster/join endpoint + the UI shape fix makes the button return `{"ok": true}` and paint "Paired successfully!" in green, after which the two machines cannot authenticate to each other and hold addresses that cannot reach each other. That is precisely the visible-but-dead outcome this codebase keeps producing — a stronger version of today's state, because today's 422 at least tells the truth.
challenge.reason:
"exists_today" holds — I confirmed the queen-side handshake is real and reachable (localhost-gated mint at cluster.py:397-419, validate+accept at 421-452, cluster_pairing.py:55-205), `request_pairing` (cluster_pairing.py:206-278) has zero callers repo-wide (ripgrep across the tree, excluding build/ and worktrees — only the def and docstrings match), and cluster.js:301 does post `{queen_address, token}` to a PairRequest model requiring `pairing_token`/`instance_id`, so the 422 is real. The recommend (WIRE, not CUT) and the effort band (days) both survive.

What fails is "missing" and, consequently, "slice_boundary". The missing list stops at the transport (no endpoint, wrong UI shape) and treats everything past the handshake as working. It isn't: the secret the handshake hands back is unusable by construction (double-hash + raw secret discarded at the drone), and both recorded addresses are wrong without Tailscale, including a `.get(key, default)` that throws away the address the user typed. So the "smallest slice that leaves the product WORKING" is not the join endpoint — that slice ends in a green success toast over a cluster that 401s on its next call.

Corrected slice boundary: one slice, and its exit criterion is a live cross-machine fact, not a toast. Include (a) the drone-side POST /cluster/join calling request_pairing, (b) the UI shape fix, (c) drone persists the RAW cluster secret and `_get_auth_headers` sends it (queen keeps storing the hash), (d) address resolution falls back to a LAN IP and honours the user-supplied queen address over the queen's self-report, (e) don't rotate an existing cluster secret. Ship it when the drone's heartbeat appears in the queen's GET /cluster/peers with a fresh `last_heartbeat` from the OTHER machine — that is the first point at which "genuinely paired" is true. Items (c)-(e) are small in code; the honest cost is that none of it can be verified on one box, so budget the verification pass on two machines, which is what keeps this at the upper end of "days" rather than hours.

Note on effort confidence: the unit's HIGH confidence was set against an incomplete gap list. With the credential-storage change included it's still days, but it is no longer a pure wiring job — it changes what gets persisted, so it needs a config migration check on any box that already has a `cluster_secret_hash` in cluster_config.json.

Also endorsing the unit's own guard: leave /pairing/* alone this slice. That judgement was right and the extra defects reinforce it — do not maintain two trust systems while one of them still can't authenticate.

title: Authenticated offload over the wire
user_value: The gaming PC can be exposed to the LAN without becoming an open prompt-execution endpoint for anything on the network, and the laptop's requests are accepted rather than 401'd.
exists_today: A working auth story on the /cluster/* surface (Bearer + cluster_secret_hash, cluster.py:101-140) and a general remote-auth middleware with token check, endpoint allowlist, rate limiting and audit (main.py:1216+).
missing: run_completion_cluster builds its request with only Content-Type (inference_router.py:644-649) and targets the peer's /v1/chat/completions, which sits behind the remote middleware. So the drone has exactly two states, both wrong: remote_enabled=true → every offload 401s (and today that 401 becomes the user's answer), or remote_enabled=false → unauthenticated LAN prompt execution. Needs the paired cluster secret attached as a Bearer header, and /v1/chat/completions confirmed present in the remote endpoint allowlist.
depends_on: Real pairing handshake (unit 4) — there is no shared secret to send until the two machines have genuinely paired.
slice_boundary: Send the header and verify a 401 now degrades to local silently (which unit 1 guarantees). Do not build a bespoke cluster transport; reuse the existing remote-auth path so there is one auth story, not three.
area: CLUSTERING — potato→gaming-PC inference offload, drone side, pairing UX, discovery

title: Make the drone reachable, and stop advertising an address we aren't listening on
user_value: The user turns the gaming PC into a drone with one switch instead of discovering, by trial and error, that they must launch it with an undocumented --host flag. Discovery stops promising peers that cannot be connected to.
exists_today: serve.py:72 already supports --host / LAYLA_HOST; mDNS advertises the detected LAN IP and port (mdns_discovery.py:274, 293); check_peer_health pings /health (mdns_discovery.py:416-446) and is already surfaced at GET /pairing/peer/{id}/health.
missing: Default bind is 127.0.0.1 (serve.py:125), so a stock drone is unreachable — while mDNS advertises its LAN IP anyway, so peers see an entry that can never connect. Needs: the advertised address derived from the actual bind, a refusal (or loud warning) to advertise externally while bound to loopback, and a 'Act as a drone for other devices' action that sets the bind and the required flags together. DEPLOYMENT.md:304 mentions --host 0.0.0.0 only in an unrelated context.
depends_on: NONE for the advertise-truth fix; the one-switch drone mode should follow the pairing fix (unit 4) so the switch has something to enable.
slice_boundary: Split it: (a) advertise only what we bind, and mark unreachable peers in the existing peers list — pure truth, ships alone; (b) the drone-mode switch, after pairing works. Never ship (b) first.

challenge.reason: exists_today is verified real (start_service has live callers at main.py:421-424 startup and pairing.py:216/404; the Ping button in pairing.js:101/319 reaches check_peer_health via pairing.py:387), and the BUILD recommendation plus the (a)-before-(b) split survive. But 'missing' understates the gap in a way that would make the fix wrong as written. (1) The app process has NO knowledge of its bind address — there is no 'host' key in config_schema.py, runtime_safety.py, or main.py; LAYLA_HOST exists only inside serve.py:93. Dockerfile:31 and ~15 docs launch `uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0` with LAYLA_HOST unset, so deriving the advertised address from env/config would silence advertising on every correctly-configured remote deployment — inverting the bug. The fix requires introspecting the real bound socket (uvicorn Server.servers[*].sockets) or serve.py exporting it, with a fallback that does not false-negative under Docker. (2) The advertised PORT is a second lie the unit never mentions: mdns_discovery.py:271 uses cfg.get('port', 8000) while serve.py:99 lets port_guard relocate to a free port (port_guard.py:89-98) — the common Windows case. Slice (a) must include this or it still ships a lie. (3) check_peer_health has no automatic caller and GET /pairing/peers (pairing.py:344) exposes no reachability field, so 'mark unreachable peers' is new work, and a naive sequential sweep at timeout=3.0 blocks a request thread on a CPU-only box (needs concurrency + caching). Effort: 'days' stays honest for the corrected (a), but (b) is understated on Windows — binding 0.0.0.0 does not open inbound 8000; a true one-switch drone needs an elevated firewall rule (netsh advfirewall) or it under-delivers silently.
challenge.hidden_dependency: The server process cannot currently observe its own bind address — 'host' appears nowhere in config_schema.py, runtime_safety.py, or main.py, and LAYLA_HOST is local to serve.py:93 while Dockerfile:31 and the documented launch commands bind 0.0.0.0 without it. A bind-truth mechanism (uvicorn socket introspection or an explicit export from serve.py, with a Docker-safe fallback) must land before any advertise-what-you-bind check, or the check will suppress advertising on correctly-configured remote hosts. Coupled to this: the advertised port comes from config (mdns_discovery.py:271) and ignores port_guard relocation (port_guard.py:89-98), so the same slice must carry the effective port too.

title: Discovery that says so when it cannot work
user_value: A user whose install lacks zeroconf sees 'LAN discovery unavailable — zeroconf not installed' in the cluster panel instead of an empty peer list that looks like 'no other devices found'. Right now the two states are indistinguishable, which is exactly how a missing dependency survives unnoticed.
exists_today: start_service handles the ImportError cleanly and warns (mdns_discovery.py:257-261); _is_zeroconf_available exists (mdns_discovery.py:464); is_running() and get_status() are already exposed via GET /pairing/status. zeroconf is correctly declared in agent/requirements.txt:217 and pyproject.toml:164.
missing: PROBED: zeroconf is NOT installed in C:\Work\Programming\Layla\.venv, so on this very box discovery is inert and the failure is invisible — main.py:429-430 logs it at debug. Needs the dependency actually installed (and covered by the installer profile that claims clustering), plus the unavailable/failed reason propagated into the status payload and the cluster panel. Worth checking whether any install profile omits it deliberately.
slice_boundary: Surface the reason in status + panel and fix the install. This is the cheapest unit here and it protects every unit above from being debugged blind.

title: The actual speedup: offload the streaming path
user_value: THE point of the whole area. The user asks a question on the laptop and the answer streams back at gaming-PC speed instead of the ~14s-first-token CPU floor. Until this lands, every other unit makes clustering correct and safe but still not faster on the path users actually use.
exists_today: run_completion_cluster already implements streaming: it posts stream=true and parses the SSE delta frames into token strings (inference_router.py:654-673) — the exact shape _counting_gen consumes. The non-stream hook and its fallback contract are in place at llm_gateway.py:1050-1062.
missing: The hook sits below the streaming early-return (llm_gateway.py:1003-1033), so the default path never reaches it. Needs the peer attempt hoisted above the stream branch, returning the peer's generator when the first token arrives in time and falling back to a local generator otherwise — first-token timeout, not total timeout, or a slow peer is worse than no peer. The stream generator's failure path (line 670-672) currently swallows the error and yields "", which would surface as a blank reply; it must fail before the first token is emitted so fallback is still possible.
depends_on: Units 1, 3, 5 (truthful failure, paired-only selection, auth) — hoisting this first would put the unsafe, prompt-leaking, error-string-emitting path onto every user turn.
slice_boundary: Streaming offload with first-token fallback, still default-off, verified on two real machines. This is the first slice where the user can actually observe the feature working — and it must be the LAST of the correctness units, not the first.

challenge.reason: The structural claim is accurate and verified: the cluster hook at llm_gateway.py:1050 sits below the streaming early-return at :1002-1032, so the default path never reaches it; run_completion_cluster's SSE parsing (inference_router.py:652-670) and its error-swallowing `yield ""` (:670-672) are exactly as described; and the stated dependency on paired-only selection is real (get_best_peer_for_inference at mdns_discovery.py:392-405 applies no pairing filter at all, unlike the unused _get_cluster_peers at :598-611). But 'missing' is not the whole gap. run_completion_cluster posts to the peer's /v1/chat/completions, which is not an inference primitive — it is the full agent pipeline (routers/openai_compat.py:191+): content guard over aggregate messages, _quick_reply_for_trivial_turn short-circuit, stream_reason with the PEER's own persona/aspect/memory, strip_junk_from_reply, and get_append_history() writing to the peer's DB. The local path being replaced is llm.create_completion(prompt, ...) (inference_router.py:316), a raw completion over an ALREADY fully-rendered prompt. Offloading therefore hands a finished prompt to a peer as a bare user message, and the peer re-wraps it in its own system prompt and memory. The user would not see the same answer faster — they would see a different, double-wrapped answer from the peer's brain, plus 400s when an internal prompt trips the content guard, phatic quick-replies on goal-looking-trivial prompts, and history rows polluting the peer. On the streaming path that divergence is the visible reply, which is precisely the visible-but-wrong outcome this challenge defaults against. Two smaller omissions: hoisting above :1002 must preserve _add_usage and the _routing_prompt_var.reset(routing_tok) currently in _counting_gen's finally, or an early return leaks the contextvar; and first-token liveness cannot key off the peer's first SSE frame, since the role-delta and layla_progress frames carry no content and the parser correctly yields nothing for them. Also worth noting the streaming branch of run_completion_cluster has never had a live caller — try_cluster_offload_first hardcodes stream=False (:829) and run_completion_with_cluster documents itself as having no entry point (:709-712) — so 'exists_today' means 'present and plausible', not 'exercised'. Still worth building; it needs to absorb a prerequisite unit first.
challenge.hidden_dependency: No raw-inference endpoint exists on the peer. The only inference entry a Layla instance exposes is /v1/chat/completions, which runs the entire agent pipeline (content guard, quick-reply short-circuit, peer's own persona/memory via stream_reason, junk-stripping, conversation-history append). A new authenticated peer endpoint taking prompt/max_tokens/temperature/stop and returning raw tokens with no pipeline — plus its client — must land before offload can return text comparable to the local create_completion path. This prerequisite also retroactively applies to the already-shipped non-streaming hook, which has the same semantic mismatch today (masked only by being default-off).

title: Background batch offload — the work queue is for jobs, not for chat
user_value: Long jobs the user never watches — document ingestion, study sessions, wiki builds, backups — run on the gaming PC instead of stealing the laptop's four cores mid-conversation. A genuine second win, and the one this subsystem is actually shaped for.
exists_today: A complete receive side: POST /cluster/task/submit with real Bearer auth (cluster.py:201-224), a persistent WorkUnit queue (work_unit.py), DroneWorker/QueenWorker claim-and-execute loops started under cluster_enabled (main.py:553-558), and handlers already written for ingestion, study, backup, consolidation and wiki_build (drone_worker.py:238-319).
missing: No client ever submits: cluster_network.submit_task carries a verified NO ENTRY POINT note at cluster_network.py:420 and has zero non-test callers. And _handle_inference cannot succeed even if called — drone_worker.py:217 calls result.split() on the dict run_completion returns, so it raises and returns {"error": ...} every time, while also stuffing the raw OpenAI envelope into "output". Needs a real submitter on one job type end-to-end. Note this path is poll-based and is the WRONG mechanism for interactive chat — do not let it compete with unit 8; drop or fix-and-ignore _handle_inference and let the /v1 path own inference.
depends_on: Units 4 and 5 (pairing + auth) — the submit endpoint requires the cluster secret.
slice_boundary: Pick ONE job type (document ingestion is the most visible and the most CPU-hungry) and wire submit → poll → collect for it alone, with local execution as the fallback when no drone is paired. Do not wire all seven handlers at once. Defer this entire unit until the interactive path in units 1-9 actually works — it is the second win, not the first.

challenge.reason: Recommendation (DEFER) is correct, but exists_today is materially false and would mislead a planner. AST walk over 946 files (with backup_database + ingest_file asserted as present controls) shows the handlers are NOT "already written": `process_file` (drone_worker.py:246, imported for ingestion) has ZERO definitions anywhere — the real API is `ingest_file(Path, *, topic="") -> IngestResult`, a dataclass not a dict — so _handle_ingestion always returns {"error":"ingestion_pipeline_not_available"}. `autonomous_wiki_entry` (drone_worker.py:311) also has ZERO definitions; wiki.py exports build_candidate/write_wiki_entry/merge_wiki_markdown, so wiki_build always returns {"error":"wiki_module_not_available"}. _handle_study (drone_worker.py:259) has the same bug class as _handle_inference — it stuffs the raw {"choices":[...]} envelope into {"summary": result} without raising, so it returns SUCCESS with garbage, which is worse than the failure the unit did catch. Only backup and consolidation resolve to real symbols. The unit correctly diagnoses _handle_inference (run_completion's docstring at llm_gateway.py:860 confirms the dict return) and correctly verifies submit_task has zero non-test callers (only hits: the NO-ENTRY-POINT comment at cluster_network.py:420, main.py:542, inference_router.py:712, and tests). But `missing` names only the client-side hole plus one handler bug, when the receive side is itself hollow — so the slice_boundary, which picks document ingestion precisely because it is "most visible", targets the one job type whose handler cannot execute. Wiring submit->poll->collect for it as written yields a paired drone that accepts jobs and reports failure: visible-but-dead, the exact defect the challenge is meant to block. The slice must first repair _handle_ingestion against the real ingest_file signature/result shape and prove local-execution fallback, before any submitter exists. Effort "weeks" appears to bundle units 4-5; isolated to this unit post-pairing/auth it is submitter + poller + collector + fallback + three handler repairs + a two-machine LAN test = days, with the second physical box (not the code) being the real friction. Also for operator awareness, not acted on: cluster_network.py:420 records a prior architecture review whose verdict on this ~4,600 LOC subsystem was CUT, arguing distributed inference never beats a model that fits in RAM — that argument targets inference offload, not batch offload, so it does not defeat this unit, but it should be weighed before funding the pillar.
challenge.hidden_dependency: The receive-side handlers are not actually functional. Three of five cited handlers reference symbols or shapes that do not exist: `process_file` (0 defs; ingestion), `autonomous_wiki_entry` (0 defs; wiki_build), and `_handle_study` silently returns the raw OpenAI envelope. Repairing _handle_ingestion against layla/ingestion/pipeline.py's real `ingest_file(Path, *, topic="") -> IngestResult` is a hard prerequisite of the chosen slice, and it must land BEFORE any client submitter — otherwise the first thing a paired drone does is accept a job and fail it.
