challenge.reason: Central evidence is confounded by run order. The unit argues the occupancy gate "has still never fired" and cites conversation e11e5452 (18 messages) as proof the ring should have crossed the 16 threshold. But e11e5452's last message is 2026-07-16T10:40, while the occupancy gate landed in commit 2fbc4f5 on 2026-07-21 — five days later. That conversation ran entirely against the OLD unreachable token-pressure gate and says nothing about the new code. Live DB (read-only): every one of the ten conversations with messages at/after 2026-07-21 has exactly 2 messages. Peak post-fix ring occupancy is 2 against a threshold of 16. The branch has not fired because nothing has approached it, not because it is defective — there is currently zero evidence in either direction, so the framing "either the ring never reached 16 or the summarizer degraded" is a false dichotomy over an untested code path. Two further claims are wrong: (1) is_memory_junk does NOT reject the summary (probed with a realistic "[Earlier conversation summary]" payload -> False), and add_timeline_event->int / create_episode->str match their call sites, so no silent swallower or signature mismatch exists; (2) intelligence.js:106 renders an honest empty state ("Nothing yet — milestones and events show here as you work together"), so the TRUTH BEFORE EXPOSURE violation is overstated — the card is truthful, not visible-but-lying. The slice_boundary is also unshippable as written: "add a counter and wait for the next real long conversation" concedes an indefinite wait at the observed 2-message-per-conversation rate, when an 8-turn conversation exercises the gate deterministically today. Verified-correct parts: the gate at shared_state.py:162, the revision guard, and append_conv_history's 7 live call sites in routers/agent.py are all real and user-reachable.
challenge.hidden_dependency: The LLM-lock admission race, not the ring. _compress_to_summary (context_manager.py:212) does a NON-BLOCKING acquire on llm_serialize_lock, which llm_gateway.py:85 aliases to a plain RLock — reentrant only for its own holder. The compaction daemon spawned at shared_state.py:192 is a DIFFERENT thread, so that acquire fails whenever any generation is in flight. It is spawned at the tail of an assistant turn on a CPU-only box where turns take ~70-115s. If the streaming generator at routers/agent.py:914 has not fully released the lock, or the next turn starts first, _compress_to_summary raises RuntimeError("llm busy"), degrades to "[Earlier conversation (truncated)]", _persisted_kind is False, and the force path bails at context_manager.py:117 on EVERY attempt — five stores stay permanently empty while every component looks healthy and the debug log is the only trace. This, not ring occupancy, is the mechanism most likely to keep the branch dead once conversations do get long enough, and the unit does not mention it at all.

TRUTH BEFORE EXPOSURE forbids surfacing dead features; it does not require shipping a stage with no surface. title: config_migrator has zero callers — old installs never get migrated
user_value: A user upgrading from an older Layla gets their runtime_config.json brought forward automatically instead of silently running on a config missing every key added since they installed.
exists_today: agent/services/infrastructure/config_migrator.py:90 `migrate_config(cfg) -> (cfg, changes)` is complete, with a _NEW_DEFAULTS table and logging at lines 122-124. 9 tests pass (tests/test_config_migrator.py).
missing: Zero callers. A grep for migrate_config across all .py returns only the module's own docstring example (line 10-11), its definition, its internal use at line 134, and the test file. It is never invoked at startup or on config load.
slice_boundary: The honest question first: runtime_safety.load_config() already applies a large default table, so the migrator may be genuinely redundant rather than merely unwired — in which case this is a CUT, not a WIRE. One probe settles it: load an old config snapshot (backups/ or runtime_config.json.bak-p13 exists) through load_config() and diff against migrate_config()'s output. If load_config already covers it, delete the module and its tests. If it does not, call migrate_config once inside load_config's cold path. Either way the slice is self-contained and the product is working before and after.

challenge.hidden_dependency: Three things the unit missed. (1) The proposed WIRE branch was inert by construction: load_config() returns an in-memory dict and never writes back to disk, so calling migrate_config in its cold path would persist nothing — deprecated keys would be stripped from memory every load and remain on disk forever. (2) agent/first_run.py:84 and agent/services/infrastructure/setup_engine.py:118 still WRITE both deprecated keys (knowledge_unrestricted, anonymous_access) into every newly generated config; the migrator was never what kept them out. Those keys have zero readers repo-wide, so removing them is cosmetic — but if done, it must be done at those two write sites, not via the migrator. (3) config_migrator._NEW_DEFAULTS is a hand-synced duplicate of runtime_safety.load_config's defaults, flagged by its own comment ('Defaults here MUST match runtime_safety.py defaults dict'). It is a second source of truth for 40 keys — an argument for deletion, not retention. The 9 passing tests only exercise the migrator against itself and never reference load_config, which is why they never surfaced the redundancy.
challenge.reason: Probe settles it: the migrator is REDUNDANT, not merely unwired — so this is a CUT, and the unit's user_value claim is false. Probe (agent/, .venv-test): pointed runtime_safety.CONFIG_FILE at a temp file containing only {max_cpu_percent: 80, knowledge_unrestricted: true, anonymous_access: true}, cleared the module caches (_config_cache/_config_mtime/_config_last_check), and called load_config(). Precondition asserted so it could not silently measure the live config: assert cfg['max_cpu_percent'] == 80. Resolved path printed: C:\\Users\\minam\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\tmp8drq_dtb\\runtime_config.json. Result: load_config returned 432 keys; MISSING from _NEW_DEFAULTS = 0 of 40; VALUE MISMATCHES vs migrator = {} (empty). An earlier version of this probe read the live 437-key config and was discarded as confounded. Breakdown of what migrate_config actually contributes: _RENAMES is an empty dict with only a commented placeholder (step 1 is a structural no-op); _NEW_DEFAULTS — the module's entire substance, 40 keys — is 100% covered by load_config with identical values; _DEPRECATED removes 2 keys that ripgrep shows have zero readers anywhere in the repo (only writers: first_run.py:84, setup_engine.py:118, and runtime_config.example.json:12-13). Net behavioral delta of wiring it in: zero. Therefore 'exists_today' is real as code but the feature it claims to enable does not exist as a gap: a user upgrading from an old install is already fully served at runtime by load_config, so nothing is 'silently missing'. 'missing' (zero callers) is accurate but is not the whole gap — the whole gap is that there is nothing to call it FOR. Effort 'hours' is wrong: the probe took minutes and the deletion is minutes. INVESTIGATE was a defensible posture and the slice_boundary correctly anticipated the CUT branch and named the right probe, but the investigation is now complete, so carrying this as an open roadmap item with 'hours' of effort and a false user-value statement would misprice it. Shippability: deleting agent/services/infrastructure/config_migrator.py plus agent/tests/test_config_migrator.py leaves the product working before and after and removes a duplicated second source of truth for 40 config defaults; nothing becomes visible-but-dead, since nothing was ever visible. Confidence HIGH on redundancy (direct executed probe with asserted precondition, exact value comparison), HIGH on zero-readers for the deprecated keys (ripgrep across py/js/json).

title: Dead UI controls: IndexedDB cache toggle, ingest label field, unpersisted composer prefs
user_value: Three small honesty repairs. The user stops seeing a checkbox that does nothing, an input field whose value is thrown away, and a composer setting that forgets itself on reload.
exists_today: All three controls render and all three have registered handlers — this is the semantic form of the defect, not the wiring form. (1) #idb-cache-toggle → main.js:560 saveIdbCache writes localStorage 'layla_idb_cache'; the ONLY reader is obsidian.js:112, which restores the checkbox's own state. perf.js:24 opens indexedDB unconditionally and never consults the key. A checkbox that only remembers itself. (2) #km-label (index.html:986, 'Label (optional)') — never referenced in any JS file; settings-full.js:854 POSTs only {directory: path} to /intelligence/kb/build/directory. (3) main.js:550 savePipelineMode and chat-render.js:189 both write localStorage keys ('layla_engineering_pipeline_mode', 'layla_compose_open') that NOTHING reads.
missing: Consumers. Note the near-miss that makes this worth stating carefully: #engineering-pipeline-mode is NOT dead — app.js:365-366 reads it live and puts it in the /agent payload. Only its persistence is dead. My localStorage probe would have called it a dead control; reading the send path corrected that. Same for #plan-mode-toggle and #reasoning-effort (app.js:361-364) — both genuinely wired.
slice_boundary: Split by verdict, one commit: CUT #idb-cache-toggle and #km-label from index.html (nothing consumes either, and a removed control cannot lie); WIRE the two persistence keys by restoring them in obsidian.js restorePersistedSettings() alongside the low_fx/autoscan restores that already work there. Product is strictly better and smaller. If the ingest label is actually wanted, that is a backend change to /intelligence/kb/build/directory first — TRUTH BEFORE EXPOSURE says remove the field now and re-add it when the endpoint accepts it.

title: Transports and Discord have no in-app surface — start, stop, or status
user_value: A user can see whether their Slack/Telegram/Discord bridge is running and turn it on from the app, instead of discovering from a README that they must run `python -m transports.slack_bot` in a second terminal and keep it alive themselves.
exists_today: The transports themselves are real and share one bridge: transports/base.py (call_layla_async, check_transport_inbound, allowlist + /pair pairing policy at line 81, POST /learn/ passthrough at line 310), transports/slack_bot.py, transports/telegram_bot.py, discord_bot/ (bot.py, run.py, guild_config.py, installer.py, rich_embeds.py). Credential config keys are all shipped and secret-masked (runtime_safety.py:126-127, 709-711).
missing: No caller inside the app. Nothing under agent/ imports transports.*; the only invocation documented anywhere is the module docstrings' own `python -m transports.slack_bot` (slack_bot.py:9, telegram_bot.py:8). No process supervision, no health surface, no settings panel, no entry in the UI's 109 endpoints. The operator has named this pillar as WANTED, so the code is a foundation, not a false start — it is a missing last mile.
slice_boundary: TRUTH BEFORE EXPOSURE: do NOT ship a Connections panel with start/stop buttons first. Slice 1 is read-only status — a backend endpoint that reports, per transport, whether a credential is configured and whether a process is currently reachable, surfaced as a small honest panel. That is a working product improvement on its own (today the answer is unknowable). Slice 2 adds supervised start/stop once the status signal is proven real. Reversing that order reproduces exactly the defect this audit exists to find.

challenge.hidden_dependency: Liveness is not observable by any mechanism that exists today, so Slice 1 cannot be built as specified. All three transports are outbound-only clients with no listening port (slack_bot.py:80 SocketModeHandler, telegram_bot.py:96 app.run_polling(), discord_bot/bot.py gateway), so the app cannot probe them. The reverse channel carries no identity either: transports/base.py:249 sets only Content-Type, and AgentRequest (agent/schemas/requests.py:14-40) has no source/transport field, so a stamped value would be dropped by Pydantic anyway. A "process reachable" indicator therefore requires FIRST editing transports/base.py to stamp transport identity (or emit a heartbeat) plus a server-side last-seen store. Second dependency: only discord_bot_token is in EDITABLE_SCHEMA (config_schema.py:411, category "integrations"); slack_bot_token, slack_app_token and telegram_bot_token are absent, appearing only in the plaintext-secret WARNING tuple at runtime_safety.py:134-136. GET /settings iterates EDITABLE_SCHEMA, so those three are neither readable nor settable in-app. Also unreported: agent/ui/components/chat-render.js:393 already advertises "Discord bot integration" statically with no availability check — the visible-but-dead surface exists in the shipped product right now.
challenge.reason: The no-caller core is CONFIRMED — nothing under agent/ imports transports.*; the only non-test importer is discord_bot/bot.py:62. WIRE and "days" are both right. The unit fails on depends_on and slice_boundary, which is what makes it unshippable as written. Slice 1 promises per-transport status including "whether a process is currently reachable", but that signal has no source: the transports are outbound-only clients the app cannot probe, and they send no identifying header or field on the one channel that does reach the app. Slice 1 as specified therefore ships a panel whose headline field reads "unknown" forever — visible-but-dead, the exact defect this audit hunts. Separately, exists_today's "credential config keys are all shipped and secret-masked" is half wrong (only discord_bot_token is editable; the cited line numbers 126-127 and 709-711 point at unrelated code — config-cache reset and background-worker defaults), so even the "configured?" half of the panel would show Slack and Telegram as unconfigurable with no in-app way to fix it. Corrected ordering, still TRUTH BEFORE EXPOSURE: (1a) delete or gate the static "Discord bot integration" claim at chat-render.js:393 — the product is lying today and the fix is independent and immediate; (1b) stamp transport identity in transports/base.py, accept it on AgentRequest, and record last-seen server-side — this is the truth layer and ships no UI; (1c) add the three missing credential keys to EDITABLE_SCHEMA's integrations category so "configured" is both readable and actionable; (2) only then the read-only status panel, which now has a real signal behind both fields; (3) supervised start/stop. Steps 1a-1c are all pre-UI, which keeps every stage a working product.

title: multi_agent is complete and correct but reachable only on GPU tiers
user_value: On the operator's actual hardware, compound multi-part requests would get decomposed and answered part-by-part instead of as one flattened reply. Right now the feature can never fire on this box.
exists_today: agent/services/planning/multi_agent.py (455 lines) is real — I checked specifically for the '2 fake placeholders' the earlier audit reported and found none remaining: should_use_multi_agent (line 405) and run_multi_agent (line 421) are both implemented, with recursion guard, decomposability check, and a >=2-subtask floor. It IS called: routers/agent.py:974-1013 imports it, emits a 'multi_agent' ux_state, and aggregates. An integration test drives the full flow (tests/integration/test_full_pipeline.py:168).
missing: Reachability, not code. The gate at multi_agent.py:412 requires cfg['multi_agent_orchestration_enabled'], and services/infrastructure/auto_tune.py sets that to False on every CPU tier (lines 116, 136, 151, 166) and True only on the two GPU tiers (181, 196). auto_tune is authoritative in load_config, so the setup_profiles 'multi_agent' kit (install/setup_profiles.py:67-71) and the 'council' marketplace kit flip a flag that auto_tune then overwrites. Per prior findings the router fast-path also shadows it. I did not measure the fast-path interaction in this audit.
depends_on: Needs a decision on whether auto_tune's CPU-tier refusal is a correct performance judgement or an over-broad default. That is a measurement question, not a code question.
slice_boundary: Do not touch the auto_tune table blind. First slice is a measurement: force the flag on with the 360M model, send a known 2-part compound prompt, and record whether the decomposed path is actually better AND affordable on this tier. If yes, the fix is a narrow tier override plus fast-path precedence; if no, the honest fix is removing the 'council' kit and the multi_agent setup profile, because both currently advertise a capability the platform will refuse. Either outcome ends a live inconsistency.

challenge.reason: Three of the four claims fail verification. (1) "missing = reachability, not code" is incomplete: the fast path at routers/agent.py:782-961 returns a StreamingResponse BEFORE the multi_agent block at 969, and is_self_contained_question (services/agent/response_builder.py:181) defaults to `return True` — so a natural compound question never reaches multi_agent ON ANY TIER, GPU included. A measured probe of 5 compound prompts found 4 unreachable; the only reachable one was a personal-data prompt multi_agent is not for. Flipping the flag would not deliver the stated user_value. (2) The prompts that DO escape the fast path escape by carrying a file path — and is_decomposable's `and`-clause regex (3+ \w+ runs per side) rejects those, because paths like agent/routers/agent.py break the word runs. The two gates are anti-correlated; this is a three-way conflict (flag + router precedence + heuristic), not one flag. (3) "An integration test drives the full flow" is overstated: tests/integration/test_full_pipeline.py:178 calls run_multi_agent directly with cfg={}, bypassing should_use_multi_agent, the flag, and the router entirely — and its own canonical prompt ("Fix the authentication bug and write unit tests…") fast-paths in production. (4) depends_on is factually wrong: there is no "council marketplace kit" — `council` is a deliberation_mode of debate_engine (constants.py:32, debate_engine.py:75), unrelated and running regardless of this flag. Only ONE setup profile touches the flag. And the "advertises a refused capability" defect is ALREADY FIXED: setup_profiles.py:67-75 carries a comment recording that exact lie being corrected, and install/feature_status.py + routers/setup_profiles.py:148 re-read load_config() and attribute the OFF to auto_tune by name — with the per-key override the unit proposes building (auto_tune_locked_keys, auto_tune_enabled=false) already implemented at feature_status.py:105. Confirmed live: tier=potato, auto_tune says False, agent/runtime_config.json says True — the inconsistency is real but honestly reported to the user. Critically, the proposed slice_boundary is a BROKEN PROBE of exactly the kind that produced ~10 silent lies this session: "force the flag on and send a known 2-part compound prompt" would go down the fast path, never invoke multi_agent, and report "decomposition didn't help" — measuring the wrong code path with no precondition assert. Any such measurement MUST first assert is_self_contained_question(prompt) is False and should_use_multi_agent(prompt, cfg) is True, or it measures nothing.
challenge.hidden_dependency: The self-contained-question fast path (routers/agent.py:782-961, gated by services/agent/response_builder.py:137-181 which defaults to True) structurally shadows multi_agent on ALL tiers, not just CPU — so the auto_tune flag is not the binding constraint. Compounding it, is_decomposable (services/planning/multi_agent.py:76-101) rejects the path-bearing engineering prompts that are the only ones surviving the fast path, making the two gates anti-correlated. Secondary: multi_agent is wired ONLY into the streaming branch (`if stream:` at agent.py:782) — the non-streaming path has no multi-agent branch at all. Affordability: _SUBTASK_TIMEOUT_S=120 (multi_agent.py:198) against a ~70s warm turn on this potato tier, two subtasks under the LLM lock, suggests auto_tune's CPU refusal is a correct performance judgement rather than an over-broad default.

title: tui.py — 491-line documented surface with no owner
user_value: None currently claimed. Either the terminal UI is a supported way to talk to Layla, or the README stops offering it.
exists_today: agent/tui.py, 491 lines. Publicly documented at README.md:351 (`cd agent && python tui.py`) and packaged as a pyproject extra (`tui`, pyproject.toml:160, included in the all-extras bundle at line 205). THIRD_PARTY.md:94 carries a licence entry for it.
missing: No importer, no test coverage in the non-test scan, and no evidence in this audit that it has been exercised against the current /agent contract — which has changed substantially (SSE streaming shape, ux_state events, tool-step payloads). A documented entry point that nothing verifies is the same trap as an unwired button, just slower to find.
slice_boundary: Cheapest honest slice: run it once against a live server and see whether it still renders a turn. If it works, add one smoke test so it cannot silently rot again. If it does not, remove the README line and the pyproject extra in the same commit — an offer withdrawn is better than an offer that fails on first contact. Low confidence deliberately: I did not run it (server start is out of scope for this audit), so I am reporting an unverified surface, not a proven defect.

challenge.reason: Three of the unit's four load-bearing claims are false, and it missed the real defect. (1) "No importer, no owner" is wrong: agent/tui.py has three callers on user paths — layla.py:291 cmd_tui() registered in the dispatch table at layla.py:396 (so the shipped `layla tui` subcommand launches it), agent/Start_Layla_Terminal.ps1:47 (health-polls the server then runs it), and agent/tests/test_architecture_boundaries.py:112 (KNOWN_ROOT_FILES governance registration). This is not an orphan. (2) The central risk — "the /agent contract has changed substantially (SSE streaming shape, ux_state events, tool-step payloads)" — is false for this caller. agent/schemas/requests.py:30 declares `stream: bool = False`, so the TUI's non-streaming default path is the supported one, and agent/routers/agent.py:1556 still returns both keys the TUI reads: `"response": response_text` and `"aspect_name": result.get("aspect_name", "Layla")`. All eight endpoints tui.py calls still exist (/agent, /wakeup, /pending, /approve, /study_plans, /system_export, /undo, /voice/transcribe — verified in routers/agent.py:346, study.py:262, approvals.py:14+19, study.py:221, session.py:83, system.py:167, voice.py:65). The SSE/ux_state additions are additive and never touched this caller. (3) The slice_boundary is not shippable: deleting the README line and pyproject extra leaves layla.py cmd_tui and Start_Layla_Terminal.ps1 live, i.e. a CLI subcommand that launches an officially unsupported UI — the exact visible-but-dead defect the challenge defaults against. (4) The real defect, missed: agent/routers/agent.py:378 does `conversation_id = (req.conversation_id or "").strip() or str(uuid.uuid4())`, and `rg conversation_id agent/tui.py` returns no matches — the TUI neither sends it nor stores it from the response, so every turn mints a fresh UUID. The router's own comment states per-turn context is scoped strictly to conversation_id, so the TUI has zero cross-turn memory. Critically, the unit's proposed test ("run it once and see whether it renders a turn") would have PASSED this, because single turns render correctly. Two of my own probes returned zero for all routes and were broken (bad decorator regex, then an over-narrow pattern); both were corrected before any conclusion was drawn. Correct framing: this is not an unverified orphan to defer, it is a reachable surface with one proven, cheap wiring bug.
challenge.hidden_dependency: layla.py:291/396 (`layla tui` CLI subcommand) and agent/Start_Layla_Terminal.ps1:47 both launch tui.py. Any removal of the README line or the pyproject `tui` extra MUST also remove these two callers in the same commit, or the product ships a CLI subcommand and a launcher script pointing at a withdrawn interface. Conversely, keeping the TUI requires threading conversation_id, because agent/routers/agent.py:378 falls back to a fresh uuid4 per turn when the caller omits it.

title: Carry NO_FILE_ACCESS_DIRECTIVE across the stream handoff (closes (c) and (f))
user_value: When a tool cannot run, she says she could not read the file instead of inventing its headings — on the DEFAULT streaming path, which is the only path a real user takes. Today the fix ships but is inert for everyone.
exists_today: The directive text and its rationale: agent/orchestrator.py:721. Correct application on the non-streamed branch: agent/agent_loop.py:952-957. The stream handoff already carries five other pieces of state across the same seam (goal_for_stream, reasoning_mode_for_stream, precomputed_recall_for_stream, stream_workspace_root, cognition_workspace_roots_for_stream) at agent_loop.py:960-965.
missing: A sixth key on that same handoff. agent_loop.py:958 enters `if stream_final:` and _fb_prompt is dropped on the floor; routers/agent.py:1193 then calls stream_reason(goal_for_stream, context=context) with the untouched original context. Nothing in state tells the router this was a parse_failed fallback. Same shape in routers/research.py:441. Plus: replace both vacuous tests in agent/tests/test_no_fabrication_when_tools_fail.py — the AST test must assert the directive reaches the STREAM path (state key set, router appends it), not merely that the identifier appears somewhere in the file.
slice_boundary: Set state["context_suffix_for_stream"] = NO_FILE_ACCESS_DIRECTIVE in the stream_final branch; both routers append it to context before calling stream_reason. Non-streamed path untouched (already correct), so nothing regresses. Ship with the two rewritten tests in the same commit — the old ones are what let this through.

title: Give SRS grading a caller that is not the 3B model (closes (a))
user_value: Learnings stop resurfacing forever. Today every one of the operator's 35 learnings is permanently due, so "what should I review" returns the same items on every call, in the same order, indefinitely.
exists_today: The full SM-2 step: agent/layla/tools/impl/memory.py:119-188 (_sm2 + the graded branch of spaced_repetition_review). Persistence: layla/memory/learnings.py:614 set_review_state, correct and tested. Due-query: learnings.py:543.
missing: Any caller that supplies (learning_id, quality). The only one is the model electing to call the tool with both args, which has never happened: 0 of 35 rows scheduled, review_reps 0 across the board. TWO extra blockers found while verifying: (1) agent/layla/skills/registry.py:553 registers a skill literally named spaced_repetition_review whose tools list is ["search_memories", "memory_stats", "save_note"] — it does not include the real tool, so a model routed via the skill registry can never reach the grading path; (2) learnings.py:568 still carries a docstring asserting "There is no adaptive SM-2 path for learnings", which is now false and will mislead the next reader.
slice_boundary: Slice 1 (truth, no UI): fix the skill registry tools list and the stale docstring, then have the existing review surface grade implicitly — a reviewed item that the operator does not correct grades 4, one they correct grades 2. That alone breaks the permanent-due loop with zero new UI. Slice 2 (exposure): a review view showing what is due today with explicit 0-5 grading. Do NOT ship slice 2 first — a due-list UI over an all-NULL table is exactly the half-wired surface the ordering rule forbids.

challenge.reason: Code claims verified: _sm2 + graded branch (tools/impl/memory.py:122-188) real and correct; set_review_state (learnings.py:614) correct; skill registry:553 tools list genuinely omits the real tool; learnings.py:572 docstring genuinely stale. Live DB read-only confirms 35 learnings, 0 with next_review_at, 0 with review_reps>0, ease all 2.5 — permanent-due loop is real. The unit FAILS on slice_boundary and on 'missing' being the whole gap. Slice 1 proposes grading implicitly via 'the existing review surface', which is ui/components/verify.js -> routers/learn.py:205-239 -> services/planning/verification_queue.py. That surface (a) uses a TEXT fact_id in its own verification_queue table, not learnings.id, with no mapping back; (b) INSERTs a NEW learning on confirm (verification_queue.py:270) rather than grading an existing one, so grading there would schedule a row created one line earlier and leave all 35 permanently-due rows untouched — the stated user value would remain entirely false after Slice 1; (c) is itself dead: verification_queue has 0 rows in the live DB and submit_for_verification has zero non-test callers. Also note the old scheduler path is not an alternative: services/background_intelligence.py and run_spaced_repetition_review no longer exist in the tree. A corrected Slice 1 must anchor on a surface that actually carries learnings.id — routers/memory.py:59-60 (memory browser, already lists learnings and is user-reachable) or an implicit grade at retrieval in services/prompts/system_head_builder.py:163 (no UI at all, pure truth slice). Keep the registry + docstring fixes in Slice 1 regardless; they are correct and cheap. Effort 'days' and WIRE both survive, but the days now buy a caller AND its producer, not a two-line hook onto an existing one.
challenge.hidden_dependency: The proposed Slice 1 host (verify.js / /verify/answer / services/planning/verification_queue.py) is a dead UI over an empty table: 0 rows in verification_queue, zero non-test callers of submit_for_verification, and its fact_id is a TEXT id in a separate table rather than a learnings.id — answering it INSERTs a new learning (verification_queue.py:270) instead of grading an existing one. Wiring SM-2 there would be a no-op for all 35 permanently-due learnings. A caller bound to learnings.id (routers/memory.py memory browser, or implicit grade-on-retrieval at services/prompts/system_head_builder.py:163) is required first.

title: Decide answer_quality's fate — it is a no-op under every shipped default (closes (e))
user_value: Either she starts flagging ungrounded answers and escalating the ones she is unsure of, or the operator stops believing a quality signal exists. Right now P13-C1b's "answer_quality now reaches the streamed path" is true and buys nothing.
exists_today: assess_answer (services/llm/answer_assessment.py:17) is complete. Its call site in turn_commit.py:445-452 is correctly on the turn boundary with the cleaned reply — the C1b wiring is genuinely right. ground_answer and escalation_decision both exist.
missing: Nothing is broken; the feature is switched off. runtime_safety.py:568 defaults grounding_enabled=False; hybrid_escalation_enabled is absent from runtime_config.example.json and False in runtime_config.json. assess_answer returns its stub at line 23 and commit_turn's store condition (`grounding.enabled or escalate`) is unreachable. Confirmed empirically: 0 of the 40 most recent outcome_evaluations rows contain answer_quality. What is missing is a MEASUREMENT and a decision, not code.
slice_boundary: Measure before flipping anything: run the existing product benchmark with grounding_enabled=True and compare latency and false-abstain rate on this CPU box. grounding.py runs a retrieval pass per answer — on a potato tier that may be the reason it ships off. If the cost is acceptable, flip the default in runtime_safety.py and the example config together. If not, say so in the config comment so the next audit does not re-file this as a bug.

title: Apply P13-E1's argument binding to core.executor.run_tool (the real residual behind claim (d))
user_value: A tool call with one invented parameter name stops destroying the turn — for the ~90% of tools that do NOT go through invoke_tool. E1 fixed 7 tools; every approval-gated and generic tool still has the original bug.
exists_today: invoke_tool (services/tools/tool_dispatch.py:733) — correct, complete, tested. It binds model args to the real signature, drops unknowns, reports missing required args.
missing: core/executor.py does `fn = TOOLS[tool_name]["fn"]` then `fn(**clean_args)` (executor.py:118, :140) with only the 'goal' key stripped. Every tool routed via _handle_generic — which is every approval-gated tool — still raises TypeError on a wrong parameter name. It fails CLOSED (TypeError becomes ok=False), so this is a reliability bug and not the security regression the audit described. The AST guard in tests/test_tool_args_are_untrusted_input.py:108 only parses tool_dispatch.py, so it cannot see this; and because executor binds fn to a local first, the guard's TOOLS[...]["fn"](**args) pattern would not match even if pointed at the file.
slice_boundary: Route run_tool's call through invoke_tool (or lift the binding into a shared helper both import). Widen the AST guard to walk both files and to match the bind-to-local form. Keep the fail-closed behaviour for approval-gated tools — dropping an unknown arg on a dangerous tool must still be reported in the result, not swallowed.

title: Close the git_add scope-widening drop
user_value: Prevents "stage these two files" quietly becoming "stage everything in the repo".
exists_today: git_add is registered as (repo: str, path: str = '.') and is in _EXTENDED_TOOLS, so it goes through invoke_tool.
missing: invoke_tool drops any arg not in the signature. If the model calls git_add(repo=..., files=[...]) — a plausible invention — `files` is dropped and `path` falls back to its '.' default, staging the whole tree. The dropped name is reported in result["ignored_args"] but nothing acts on it. Low severity (git_add is not dangerous, staging is reversible, require_approval=False) but it is a genuine instance of a dropped arg widening scope, which is the pattern the audit was reaching for. NOTE: I found no case of this on an approval-gated tool — that part of claim (d) is refuted.
depends_on: Same touch as the run_tool binding unit — do them together.
slice_boundary: When invoke_tool drops args for a tool that has a defaulted scope parameter, return ok=False naming the accepted parameters instead of running with the default. One rule, applied where a dropped arg could widen rather than narrow effect.

title: Bucket strategy_stats by task type so the feedback loop can actually fire (not in the audit)
user_value: "Which aspect succeeds at which kind of task" starts influencing routing. Today the loop is fully wired end to end and mathematically cannot produce an answer.
exists_today: Everything. Writers: turn_commit.py:406 and run_finalizer.py:55. Reader with a real caller: get_preferred_strategy (layla/memory/strategy_stats.py:32) called from services/planning/coordinator.py:69. Table, index and retention cap all present.
missing: The key. Both writers pass the VERBATIM goal as task_type — `_g.replace("\n"," ")[:120]`. Live table: 65 rows, every one a distinct sentence ("Read CHANGELOG.md and tell me the single word..."), max success_count 2. get_preferred_strategy demands min_samples=5 on an exact task_type match, so it returns None forever unless the operator asks a byte-identical question five times. classify_task already exists and is already used for exactly this bucketing purpose in _practice_domain_for_turn (turn_commit.py:141).
slice_boundary: Both writers call classify_task(goal) and store the bucket, keeping the raw goal in a separate column if it is wanted for debugging. Existing 65 rows are unaggregatable by construction — leave them; the retention cap will age them out. This is invisible to the user until it fires, so it is safely truth-before-exposure.

title: Fast path passes no state to commit_turn — document and close, do not build (claim (b))
user_value: None. Recommending this as work would burn a stage for zero user-visible change.
exists_today: routers/agent.py:677 calls commit_turn(..., status="fast_path") with no state= — the audit's factual claim is correct.
missing: Nothing worth adding. quick_reply_for_trivial_turn (services/agent/response_builder.py:239) fires ONLY on "ok/okay/yes/yep/no/nope", the "how are you / what's up / you good" family, and the "say exactly X" / "reply exactly X" test hooks — and only when there is no context, no image and no thinking.
